Maladaptive Strategies.value: Situations that make me feel strong emotions
I dwell on what other people have done to me

Threat.value: Being superalert or watchful or on guard
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.

Negative Appraisals.value: You can never know who will harm you
The world is a dangerous place

issues.explanation: This item implies a high level of physical reactivity to memories, which is not matched by the selected triggers (arguments, old photos, and sirens). Typically, bodily reenactment would be associated with more sensory or somatic triggers.

This item suggests a high level of self-blame, which is not clearly linked to the selected negative appraisals (fear of harm, world is dangerous, emotional numbness). While it's possible for patients to ruminate on self-blame, in this case, there's no clear connection to the trauma type or other selected items.

vignette: Mrs. Johnson, a 61-year-old married woman from North America, has been struggling to come to terms with her experiences as a police officer. Her traumatic past continues to haunt her, and she finds herself frequently reliving the memories of the frightening events that took place during her time on the force. The slightest trigger can set off a chain reaction: an argument with her husband, seeing old photographs, or even just hearing sirens in the distance. These triggers evoke a range of distressing symptoms. Mrs. Johnson becomes hypervigilant, always on the lookout for potential dangers, and irritable, leading to angry outbursts that strain her relationships. She has trouble sleeping, often lying awake at night with memories replaying in her mind like a film. These snapshots of her past are vivid and unsettling, sometimes accompanied by auditory flashbacks that transport her back to the traumatic event. In an attempt to cope with these overwhelming emotions, Mrs. Johnson avoids crowded places and situations that might trigger strong feelings. She steers clear of emotional stress, opting for a more numb existence rather than risking another painful episode. Her worldview has become increasingly bleak: she genuinely believes that the world is a treacherous place, where harm can come from anyone at any time. This pessimistic outlook only serves to perpetuate her avoidance behaviors and further entrench her negative thoughts. As she dwells on past injustices, Mrs. Johnson's sense of emotional numbness grows. She feels dead inside, disconnected from the world around her. Her relationship with her husband suffers as a result, with her irritability and withdrawal causing tension between them. Despite her best efforts to move forward, Mrs. Johnson remains stuck in this cycle of fear, avoidance, and rumination. Her memories continue to haunt her, and her negative appraisals color every aspect of her life. As she navigates this complex web of emotions and behaviors, it becomes clear that a comprehensive approach will be needed to help Mrs. Johnson break free from the grip of her traumatic past and rebuild a more fulfilling life.
